style: Dry red, medium/full to full body, black fruit, pepper/spice, medium+ tannin. Climate expression varies dramatically.

nose: Pepper and spice (white or black pepper)
palate: Pepper and spice on the palate
conclusions: Moderate to long-term ageing (3-10+ years depending on site)

common_student_errors: Calling all pepper oak-derived (some is varietal)
Ignoring climate differences and concluding all Syrah tastes the same
Confusing Syrah and Shiraz as different wines
Missing the structure under ripe fruit

misconceptions_addressed.misconception: Pepper in wine is always oak-derived
misconceptions_addressed.correction: Syrah's pepper character is primary varietal expression (white pepper cool-climate; black pepper warm-climate)

misconceptions_addressed.misconception: Syrah is always spicy and jammy
misconceptions_addressed.correction: Climate dramatically affects Syrah: cool sites show pepper and fresh black fruit; warm sites show riper, jammy fruit with higher alcohol

misconceptions_addressed.misconception: Syrah and Shiraz are different grapes
misconceptions_addressed.correction: Shiraz is the Australian name for Syrah; same grape, different expression due to climate

coaching_hints: Is the fruit fresh black fruit or baked/jammy?
Identify whether pepper is white (cool) or black (warm).
What climate indicators are present in the wine?
How does alcohol level reflect the terroir?

causal_reasoning_paths.effect: Pepper and freshness retained; moderate alcohol; structured tannin
causal_reasoning_paths.example_in_wine: White pepper and herbal notes; fresh dark fruit; medium alcohol

causal_reasoning_paths.effect: Riper, jammier fruit; higher alcohol; fuller body
causal_reasoning_paths.example_in_wine: Black pepper; baked plum; 14-15% alcohol; full tannin

causal_reasoning_paths.effect: Pepper and licorice notes regardless of terroir
causal_reasoning_paths.example_in_wine: Pepper spice is consistent identifier across climates
